name: Container Security Pattern

useCases.use: All production containerised workloads deployed on Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) or Azure Container Apps
useCases.dontUse: Development-only Docker Compose environments on developer laptops with no access to production data or networks

useCases.use: CI/CD pipelines building and deploying container images to McCain's container registries
useCases.dontUse: VM-based workloads that do not use container orchestration

useCases.use: Any AKS cluster running workloads that process Confidential or Restricted data
useCases.dontUse: Temporary batch processing containers with no network exposure and ephemeral data only — if exemption approved

approvers: CCoE Security Board, Platform Engineering Lead
components: Azure Container Registry (ACR) — private container registry with geo-replication and quarantine policy
Microsoft Defender for Containers — runtime threat detection, vulnerability assessment, and Kubernetes security posture
Azure Policy (OPA Gatekeeper) — admission control policies (no root containers, no privileged pods, required labels)
Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) — managed Kubernetes with RBAC, Azure AD integration, and private cluster mode
Trivy / Microsoft Defender vulnerability scanning — image scanning in CI pipeline and registry
Azure Monitor for Containers — pod-level resource monitoring, log collection, and alerting
Azure Key Vault CSI Driver — secrets injection into pods without environment variable exposure
Falco (runtime) — kernel-level anomaly detection for container runtime threats

guardrails.rule: All container images must pass vulnerability scan with zero Critical CVEs before deployment to production
Containers must not run as root and must have read-only root filesystem where application supports it
Secrets must not be stored in container images or environment variables — Key Vault CSI Driver required
All production images must be pulled exclusively from McCain's Azure Container Registry — no external registries
Defender for Containers must be enabled on all production AKS clusters

revisionHistory.author: Sarah Chen
revisionHistory.summary: Initial container security pattern for AKS workloads

revisionHistory.author: Sarah Chen
revisionHistory.summary: Added Key Vault CSI Driver mandate, Falco runtime detection, and image signing with Notation

revisionHistory.author: Sarah Chen
revisionHistory.summary: Endorsed. Added OPA Gatekeeper admission policy library and Defender for Containers integration.

strategicIntent: Establish a secure container supply chain and runtime security posture for all containerised workloads running on Azure Kubernetes Service — from image build through registry scanning, admission control, and runtime threat detection — ensuring containers are treated as untrusted code requiring continuous verification.
exceptionProcess: Submit Container Security Exception via ServiceNow. Root container exceptions require CISO approval and compensating controls (enhanced Falco rules, network policy isolation, 24hr review cycle). Maximum exception duration 90 days.
interactionSteps: Developer builds container image — CI pipeline runs Trivy vulnerability scan; build fails on Critical or High CVEs without approved exception
Image pushed to Azure Container Registry with quarantine policy — Defender for Containers rescans the image in registry before promoting to production repository
Deployment manifest submitted to AKS — Azure Policy / OPA Gatekeeper admission controller validates: no root user, no privileged containers, required labels present, image from approved ACR only
Pod starts — Key Vault CSI Driver mounts secrets as files (no env vars); containers run as non-root with read-only filesystem where possible
Defender for Containers monitors runtime behaviour — anomalous syscalls, privilege escalation attempts, or unexpected network connections trigger Sentinel alerts
Falco rules detect known attack patterns (shell spawned in container, sensitive file access, crypto mining signatures) and emit events to Sentinel
Azure Monitor collects pod logs and metrics; alerts on resource exhaustion, crash loops, and OOM events via action group
problemStatement: Container images introduce a novel software supply chain attack surface: vulnerable base images, embedded secrets, missing CVE patches, and unrestricted container privileges can all be exploited at runtime. Without a standardised container security pattern, teams ship unscanned images with excessive privileges and no runtime threat detection.
securityConsiderations: All container images must originate from approved base images on the McCain approved image list — no public Docker Hub pulls in production
Containers must not run as root (UID 0) in production — runAsNonRoot must be enforced via OPA Gatekeeper policy
No secrets may be injected via environment variables — all secrets must use Key Vault CSI Driver or Managed Identity
AKS clusters must operate in private cluster mode — no public API server endpoint in production
Container image signatures must be verified at admission using Notation and Azure Key Vault-backed signing keys
